### Post by broken bot on 2009-04-21
I just upgraded to ubuntu and it gave me 800 x 600 but that was very low and unwanted. Then when I upgraded it downsized it to 640x480....

how do i get it to 1024x786 (desired)
or 800x600.

ive been reading about changing the xorg config or something like that but it wont give me permission to do so.


Xorg  below

```
 
# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.

# in this file, now are automatically configured by the server and settings
# here are ignored.
#

# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Monitor		"Configured Monitor"
	Device		"Configured Video Device"
	DefaultDepth	24
	Option	"AddARGBGLXVisuals"	"True"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load	"glx"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Configured Video Device"
	Option		"UseFBDev"		"true"
	Driver	"nvidia"
	Option	"NoLogo"	"True"
EndSection

```

Thanks in advance to anyone that helps or solves my problem.

### Post by CatKiller on 2009-04-21
> **broken bot said:**
> ive been reading about changing the xorg config or something like that but it wont give me permission to do so.

Then you've not been reading enough :)

Normally, resolution problems are caused by one of the monitor, the graphics card, or the graphics driver not passing the EDID information to X that lets it automatically set the correct refresh rate, and so it defaults to really low values that won't damage any monitors. You can manually set the values for your monitor by putting the VertRefresh and HorizSync ranges in your xorg.conf. You should be able to get these numbers from the manual or specifications for your monitor. These values go in the "Monitor" Section of xorg.conf.

You can edit xorg.conf with ```
sudo cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf /etc/X11/xorg.conf.backup
gksudo gedit /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```

```
Section "Monitor"
        Identifier      "Configured Monitor"
        HorizSync       aa-bb
        VertRefresh     cc-dd
EndSection

```

You'll need to replace aa-dd with the values for **your** monitor, otherwise you could well permanently damage your monitor.

---

### Post by peyre on 2009-04-21
I had the same problem when I first switched to *buntu.  Turned out the problem was that I was hooked up to a KVM.  I plugged the monitor directly into the computer, and problem was solved.

---

### Post by Dileeshvar on 2009-04-21
yes he is right !
here is my output for you !!
```
Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Monitor0"
    VendorName     "Unknown"
    ModelName      "Unknown"
    HorizSync       30.0 - 72.0
    VertRefresh     50.0 - 160.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

```
and  u can optimize the screen resolution as done below more than one is also possible :) like "1024x768","1200x1024"
```
Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Device0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "AddARGBGLXVisuals" "True"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1024x768"

```
 

check your monitors HorizSync VertRefresh by googling and find it out this helps !!

---

	Screen	1
	Vendorname	"NVIDIA"
EndSection

	Identifier	"screen1"

	Defaultdepth	24
	Monitor		"monitor1"
EndSection
Section "monitor" #  
	Identifier	"monitor1"
	Gamma	1.0
EndSection
Section "ServerFlags"
EndSection

### Post by Dileeshvar on 2009-04-23
> **broken bot said:**
> by the way if you can.... when telling me how to change res to 1080x786 can you give me more higher resolution options 

thanks


You can do that by going to the xorg.conf file and editing the modes in the section screen to "1080x768" but before that did you find horsync and vertrefresh for your monitor and added it in the xorg.conf file ??

---
